notes: Phase 2a adjudication converts the D01 chronology's Phase-1 'disputed' verdicts into two independent failure points (GC-18-060's 457 BC anchor and GC-18-071's astronomically-excluded AD 31 crucifixion) plus one distinct frame failure (GC-18-078's discredited 'fixed beyond question' certainty-claim), with the remaining chain links (GC-18-063/065/069/073/081) inheriting the anchor's failure rather than constituting fresh errors. GC-18-078 now scores highest in this batch (4x5=20) because a discredited epistemic-absolute claim is a qualitatively harsher adjudicated finding than the anchor's merely improbable date, inverting Phase 1's ranking where the terminal date claim (GC-18-081) scored highest. GC-18-058 exits the discrepancy/vindication ledger entirely as a charter-section-11 conditional premise (the Dan 8/Dan 9 synchronism is purely hermeneutic and out of scope), though its structural centrality and load-bearing status are unchanged from Phase 1. Every item but GC-18-069 (D01's weakest link, whose Jews-first substance is independently well-supported) and GC-18-120 (a D03 corroborating showpiece outside the date chain) remains centrality 5 / load-bearing, reflecting how tightly the 1844 chronology is wound around the single 457 BC anchor.

GC-18-058.reasoning: The synchronism linking the 2300 days of Daniel 8 to the seventy weeks of Daniel 9 is the hermeneutic hinge on which every date in the chapter's chain depends; adjudication finds it entirely a prophetic-interpretive question (the chathak/'cut off' philology dispute between SDA and critical scholarship) and thus out of the charter's empirical scope, ruled neither true nor false. Structurally it remains the load-bearing joint of the argument: without asserting that the two periods 'must begin together,' the 2300-day prophecy has no stated starting point and none of the subsequent 457 BC-through-1844 dates could be computed. Per charter section 11 this is a conditional premise rather than a discrepancy or a vindication and must not be counted in either impact column downstream.

GC-18-060.reasoning: Failing_component_kind is fact: on standard Persian chronology (Parker & Dubberstein) Artaxerxes I's 7th year begins in 458 BC, so 457 requires the confessionally concentrated Tishri-reckoning defense that Parker (1955) and Stern (2000) rebutted on the Elephantine evidence, ruled improbable as written. The framing_gap of 2 means the weakened claim ('458/457 BC, within a year of the Millerite date, following the era's Ussher chronology') is probable and the substance -- a real royal rescript dated close to the true year -- survives; the as-written failure is the flat assertion of 457 as settled fact rather than a contested one-year choice. This is an independent failure point, not chain-inherited -- it is the anchor itself -- so removing it leaves the entire 483/490/1810-year chain with no fixed starting date, making it fully load-bearing for the chapter and for the book's 1844 doctrine.

GC-18-063.reasoning: Chain-inherited from the 457 BC anchor (GC-18-060) for its year component, and it additionally asserts its own fact: an 'autumn' effective date that, per the ruling, has 'no documentary basis in Ezra, Persian administrative sources, or scholarship,' a specification adjudication finds retrofitted from the required 1844 endpoint. Failing_component_kind is fact; ruled improbable as written, weakened to probable (framing_gap 2) once restated as 'practical effect from the second half of the year of Ezra's arrival' -- the substance survives, but the specific season-fixing claim does not. The year-level liability sits with GC-18-060, not this claim independently, but the added seasonal precision is this claim's own contribution and is what converts the chain's 'about 1843' into the doctrinally required 'autumn 1844,' so it remains load-bearing.

GC-18-065.reasoning: Chain-inherited from the 457 BC anchor: 'autumn AD 27' is a value generated by 457+483 rather than an independently attested date, so its failure derives from GC-18-060 rather than constituting a fresh error in New Testament chronology. Failing_component_kind is fact; ruled improbable as written (modern reconstructions run AD 28-29, with 27 a live but weak edge case surviving only via the abandoned co-regency reading), but the framing_gap of 3 is large -- weakened to 'baptized in the late AD 20s (c. 27-29)' the claim is well-supported, so the substantive core (a real baptism inaugurating the ministry) is intact and only the precise year/season is off. Load-bearing for the chapter's internal date sequence, but severity is modest since the printed value sits at the edge of, rather than outside, the evidenced range.

GC-18-069.reasoning: Chain-inherited: both endpoints (AD 27 start, AD 34 close) are generated by the 457-anchored scheme rather than independently argued, so its start-date failure traces to GC-18-060/065 rather than being a fresh error. Failing_component_kind is fact, confined to the AD 27 start; the substantive claim -- an early, Jews-first missionary phase -- is well-supported scholarship (Meier, Fredriksen) and the AD 34 close is independently genuinely-open, so the framing_gap of 3 reflects a claim whose substance survives almost intact once restated as 'late 20s to mid-30s.' This is the weakest link in the D01 cluster: it restates already-established endpoints as connective narration rather than computing anything new, so centrality and load-bearing are lower than the sibling anchor claims.

GC-18-071.reasoning: Failing_component_kind is fact, and this is an independent failure point, not inherited from the 457 anchor: astronomical-calendrical reconstruction since Fotheringham, consolidated by Humphreys & Waddington (Nature, 1983), restricts a Friday Nisan-14 crucifixion under Pilate to 7 April AD 30 or 3 April AD 33, excluding AD 31 on any evidenced Judean calendar reckoning; ruled improbable as written with high confidence. The framing_gap of 3 means the weakened claim ('crucified at a Passover in the early 30s, roughly two to three and a half years after baptism') is well-supported, so the ministry existed and ended in the right general period -- but as an independent, astronomically-testable claim it earns its own severity rather than borrowing 060's. It remains fully load-bearing: the chapter needs a fixed AD 31 so that '3.5 years after baptism' lands on a date that also sums to AD 34.

GC-18-073.reasoning: Chain-inherited: the AD 34 terminus is computed from the 457 anchor (457+490, no year zero) and the claim asserts this computed value as fixed ('as we have seen'), so its as-written failure derives from GC-18-060 rather than being independently generated. Failing_component_kind is fact; ruled improbable as written but weakened to probable (framing_gap 2) once restated as a plausible, not established, dating for Stephen's martyrdom, which independent scholarship already brackets c. 33-36 -- the one link in the chain whose computed value happens to land inside the genuinely-open mainstream window regardless of the anchor's fate. Still load-bearing as the chain's last stated terminus before the final 1810-year subtraction to 1844, though its own severity is lower than the anchor's since the specific numeric claim is not far from independently plausible.

GC-18-078.reasoning: Failing_component_kind is frame, not fact: the claim under adjudication is the epistemic-absolute assertion that 457 BC and AD 34 are 'fixed beyond question,' ruled discredited as written -- the strongest negative ruling in this batch -- because documented, published rival datings (standard 458 BC, the evangelical 445/444 BC Nehemiah-anchored school, and the critical Antiochene reading) demonstrably falsify 'beyond question' as a description of the field's state, in 1911 as much as now. This is an independent failure point distinct from the date claims themselves; the framing_gap of 4 is the widest in the dossier, meaning that once restated as 'an internally coherent reckoning with published defenders, one contested option among several rather than a fixed result' the claim is well-supported, so the underlying dates fare reasonably but the chapter's own certainty-claim about them does not. Scored highest in this batch because a frame failure of this kind is the chapter's own rhetorical certification of its entire preceding chronological argument, load-bearing for the chapter's persuasive strategy even though it introduces no new date.

GC-18-081.reasoning: Chain-inherited: the in-scope ruling reaches only the anchor-plus-arithmetic (457 BC + 2300 years, no year zero, correctly computed), explicitly leaving the year-day/non-Antiochene hermeneutic out of scope, so the as-written failure of this claim derives from GC-18-060 rather than being a fresh independent error. Failing_component_kind is fact; ruled improbable as written, weakened to well-supported (framing_gap 3) once restated as 'granting the Millerite premises and the then-received 457 BC date, the 2300 years end in 1844 -- the computation is internally exact and was correctly executed,' so the arithmetic itself is not in dispute, only the anchor it depends on. This is the terminal node the entire chain exists to reach and the direct historical premise of the book's sanctuary/investigative-judgment doctrine (chs 19-24+), so centrality and load-bearing remain maximal even though its own failure mode is identical in kind to the anchor's rather than a separate, freestanding error.

GC-18-120.reasoning: Failing_component_kind is fact (tied with frame at the ruling's cap), and this is an independent failure point outside the D01 chronology chain entirely -- it belongs to dossier D03, the Litch/Ottoman corroboration cluster, not the 457 BC-1844 date sequence. The real event (Ottoman acceptance of four-power intervention) is well documented, but 'at the very time specified' fails on dated evidence: the London Convention was signed 15 July 1840 and the ultimatum reached Muhammad Ali 16-19 August, with only a quarantined courier's arrival falling on 11 August; the 'control of Christian nations' frame likewise overstates an empire that remained sovereign until 1922. The framing_gap of 3 means the weakened claim (Ottoman dependency exposed 'within weeks' of Litch's date) is well-supported, so the substance of the illustration survives; but as a corroborating showpiece for the year-day method rather than a structural link in the 1844 date chain, it is not load-bearing -- deleting it costs a persuasive illustration while leaving the sanctuary/1844 argument, which rests on the separate Daniel chronology, structurally intact.
